headline: Tom’s answers show a partial-read risk: he repeatedly describes uncertainty about Principal scope and relies on explicit expectations, not on concrete examples.
summary_bullets: Tom can name the problem ("bar still feels fuzzy" / "visible ownership"), but he can’t yet translate Principal scope into specific, observable decisions on this team.
His cross-team behavior is constrained by trust-building: "I have not built enough trust with adjacent teams yet" and he "stayed in my lane instead of forcing cross-team decisions."
He’s waiting for certainty before questioning: "I need explicit expectations for the next cycle so I can calibrate fast," which blocks earlier alignment and harder judgment calls.
understanding_paragraph: Tom understands the direction of the feedback: Principal work here means visible ownership and higher-leverage decisions. What’s new is how directly he ties his current execution to uncertainty and trust gaps across adjacent teams, not technical capability. He also commits to a concrete artifact (a decision rule), but the conversation shows he still lacks a clear, team-specific bar for “highest-leverage” work.

axes.meaning: Weak signal: he reports hesitation and safety-bias, which can create stress through delayed alignment.
Slightly negative: he is shipping, but he over-indexes on being safe rather than leaning into higher-leverage ownership.
Defining gap: the Principal bar on this team feels fuzzy, and he needs explicit expectations to calibrate.
Worth acting on: he recognizes the ownership/judgment gap and plans a decision-rule artifact, but needs tighter feedback loops.

brutal_truth_employee: Tom is not operating with a clear Principal bar on this team: "this team's bar still feels fuzzy." He’s also choosing safe execution over leverage: "I have mostly stayed in my lane instead of forcing cross-team decisions" and "I still over-index on being safe."
brutal_truth_manager: When Tom said "this team's bar still feels fuzzy," the conversation moved quickly to behavior, but the missing piece was a concrete definition of “highest-leverage” for this team. Next time, push for one specific recent decision and ask, step-by-step, what rule he used and what he would do differently with a sharper bar.

next_actions.action: Email Tom a one-page definition of “highest-leverage Principal impact” for this team with 3 observable behaviors and 2 examples from recent work.
Schedule a 30-minute follow-up with Tom to review his chosen deliverable and the decision rule he wrote, then tighten it into a repeatable checklist.
Start the next 1:1 with: “Name one adjacent-team decision you could force this cycle; what trust signal will you use; what will you escalate if it’s unclear?”

watch_for: Before next 1:1: if Tom still can’t name a clear Principal bar in this team’s terms, treat clarity as unresolved and keep the bar-definition work active.
Within two weeks: if Tom’s decision-rule artifact doesn’t include one cross-team judgment and an escalation trigger, he is still waiting for certainty.
